evidence: Package hierarchy maps directly to domain capabilities: api (HTTP), config (grammar loading), exploration (semantic algorithms), indexer (corpus ingestion), model (domain DTOs), query (corpus access), utils (pure functions), viz (SVG rendering).

SemanticFieldExplorer acts as explicit facade: its Javadoc states 'coordination facade for ExplorationHandlers', with delegation to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator, insulating the HTTP layer from algorithm changes.

RelationType enum mixes two orthogonal concerns: matching strategy (SURFACE, DEP) alongside semantic role labels (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF). These are used in different contexts with no documented separation.
WordSketchApiServer.java line 96: '// TODO: remove in a future version after clients have migrated to /api/semantic-field/compare.' — untracked legacy alias with no issue reference or timeline.
model/package-info.java clearly documents the two sub-families of types (pure DTOs vs model objects with derived accessors) and ownership rules, which is a strong structural signal.
Main.java cleanly routes to handleBlackLabIndexCommand, handleBlackLabQueryCommand, handleBlackLabServerCommand — each with a focused scope, following a consistent command-handler pattern.
All production Java files follow consistent package naming (snake_case package segments, PascalCase class names, camelCase methods/fields).

HTTP handler classes in api/ are consistently package-private, consistently named with a domain-noun + 'Handlers' suffix (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers).

Query executor methods follow a consistent execute* verb family: executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeBcqlQuery, executeCqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos.
Config loading follows a consistent load* / fromReader* / parse* pattern in GrammarConfigLoader.

ExplorationHandlers has a boilerplate_duplication signal (per holistic_context.conventions.duplicate_clusters): the buildExploreRequest / buildSingleSeedRequest pair share 80%+ of their body but are not unified.

ExplorationHandlers uses private records ValidatedExploreRequest and ValidatedSingleSeedRequest that mirror each other structurally — same three-field shape minus one field — without a common supertype or shared extraction.

All enum types (PosGroup, RelationType, RelationEdgeType, SharingCategory) follow the same pattern: a private final String label field, a label() accessor, and toString() returning label() for API-safe serialization.

All HTTP handlers throw IllegalArgumentException for 400-class errors and RequestEntityTooLargeException for 413; HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single translation point for all five handler classes.

GrammarConfigLoader.parse() uses IOException for semantic validation errors (missing version, duplicate relation IDs, invalid positions) in addition to I/O failures — conflating two distinct failure categories under the same exception type.
config/ directory shows 'returns_null: 5' in holistic_context errors. RelationConfig.computeDeprel() returns @Nullable String; documented with @Nullable annotation and callers handle null explicitly.

CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex in constructor and has an internal Objects.requireNonNull guard in getTotalFrequency — the null is deliberately accepted for testing, documented in the code.
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor catches ErrorOpeningIndex (a RuntimeException) and converts it to IOException — explicit boundary translation with a code comment explaining why.
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern throws IOException and IllegalArgumentException; both propagate cleanly to wrapWithErrorHandling.
FetchExamplesOptions constructor: 'if (maxExamples < 1) throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)' — consistent validation throwing IAE at the boundary.

Consistent execute* verb family on QueryExecutor: executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeBcqlQuery, executeCqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos — each verb precisely matches the semantics.
handle* verbs on all HTTP handler methods (handleSketchRequest, handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleConcordanceExamples etc.) are uniform and intention-revealing.

build* is reserved for factory/assembly (buildEdges, buildFullPattern, buildBcqlResponse, buildCoreCollocates), parse* for parsing (parseQueryParams, parseSeedSet, parseConcordanceExamplesRequest), format* for serialization (formatDiscoveredNouns, formatAdjectiveProfile).

ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest: local vars 'noun' and 'adjective' are derived from generic HTTP params 'seed' and 'collocate' — the endpoint docs say seed=noun, collocate=adjective, but this mapping is a domain assumption baked silently into local naming.

FetchExamplesOptions declares 'public static final FetchExamplesOptions DEFAULT' but no production caller uses DEFAULT; ExplorationHandlers creates 'new FetchExamplesOptions(maxExamples)' every time. The constant name implies it is the recommended entry point but it is unused.

computeCollocateIntersection (MultiSeedExplorer) precisely names the algorithm; identifyCoreCollocates, filterByMinShared, buildCollocateToNounsMap (SemanticFieldExplorer) all name their phases accurately.
ValidatedExploreRequest and ValidatedSingleSeedRequest in ExplorationHandlers are private record names — descriptive and typed correctly for their scope.

QueryExecutor interface has a single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor), documented explicitly: 'The single production implementation means the interface acts primarily as a testability seam rather than an abstraction boundary.' The interface contract is honest.

SemanticFieldExplorer is documented as a 'coordination facade': it owns single-seed exploration directly and delegates multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator. Delegation is purposeful and documented.

ExplorationOptions / SingleSeedExplorationOptions / FetchExamplesOptions are three separate options records for exploration. SingleSeedExplorationOptions composes ExplorationOptions (base) with nounsPerCollocate. FetchExamplesOptions wraps a single int maxExamples. The composition is sound but FetchExamplesOptions is a thin single-field wrapper.
GrammarConfig / GrammarConfigLoader / GrammarConfigSerializer is a clean three-way split: value object, loader, serializer. Each absorbs change for a different reason.

RelationPatternBuilder is a static utility class extracted from RelationConfig so the record stays a pure data carrier. The extraction is real — callers only use the pattern-building logic in specific situations, not always.
CqlUtils: static utility with 4 pure-function methods (splitCqlTokens, substituteAtPosition, injectLemmaConstraint, extractConstraintAttribute, escapeForRegex). Cohesive, no dumping-ground smell.
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial hardcodes limit 40 for items inline: 'int limit = Math.min(40, itemsArr.size())' — no named constant, no abstraction for the cap policy.

RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern has two chained stream searches (primary then fallback) that could be simplified, but the logic pays for itself by centralising pattern resolution used by both SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator.

pom.xml declares exactly one JSON library (fastjson2 2.0.57), one logging API (slf4j-api 2.0.7) + one implementation (logback-classic 1.5.18), and one test framework (JUnit 5.9.0 + platform 1.9.0). No duplicates for any purpose.

BlackLab is pulled as two JARs: blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser. Both are used: BlackLabQueryExecutor uses the engine, and ContextualQueryLanguageParser from blacklab-query-parser is used directly in executeCqlQuery.
jspecify 1.0.0 is used throughout for @NonNull/@Nullable annotations — not dead weight.

BlackLab 4.x is resolved from 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/' — a raw GitHub URL with no SLA. The pom.xml comment documents this risk and suggests local install or private registry as mitigations.
No moment.js/dayjs style duplication patterns; logback is the sole SLF4J binding on the classpath.
fastjson2 security advisory is acknowledged as a permanent wontfix per project context.
No version conflicts visible: single BOM-like version properties block with ${blacklab.version}, ${fastjson.version}, ${junit.version} keeping all related versions synchronized.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: BlackLab is resolved from a raw GitHub URL with no availability guarantee, creating a CI reliability risk if the upstream branch or repository changes.

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern decomposes its algorithm into four named phase methods (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) — each is focused, extractable, and readable independently.

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList performs sum/min/max in a single pass and a second pass for variance; a comment explains the two-pass is unavoidable for numerical stability. The extraction level is appropriate.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens: depth-counting bracket parser handles nested brackets and quoted strings in a clean while-loop. Complexity is justified by the parsing requirement; no defensive sprawl.
GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition: single-pass character scan to find label position, then a second scan to count bracket tokens — each step is a focused loop, no premature abstraction.

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial: 'if (center == null) center = "";' — a local variable reassignment rather than a cleaner expression (e.g. Objects.requireNonNullElse). Minor style inconsistency with the null-handling conventions used elsewhere.

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial: 'int limit = Math.min(40, itemsArr.size())' — magic number 40 inline with no named constant. The equivalent constant in SketchHandlers (DEFAULT_SKETCH_RESULTS = 20, SINGLE_RELATION_RESULTS = 50) shows the established convention is to name such limits.
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates: the over-fetch factor OVER_FETCH_FACTOR = 3 is named, not inlined.

ExploreResponseAssembler.roundTo2DecimalPlaces: 'return Math.round(value * 100.0) / 100.0;' — works correctly but the idiom is slightly brittle for double precision. Could use BigDecimal.setScale or String.format if precision matters, but is consistent throughout the class.

ExploreResponseAssembler owns all model-to-presentation translation for exploration endpoints; ExplorationHandlers calls populateExploreResponse/populateComparisonResponse and handles only HTTP concerns.

Error propagation follows a single consistent path: handler methods throw IAE/RequestEntityTooLargeException → wrapWithErrorHandling in WordSketchApiServer translates to HTTP status codes. Translation happens in one place.
GrammarConfigSerializer keeps JSON serialization concern out of GrammarConfig and RelationConfig; callers in SketchHandlers import only GrammarConfigSerializer, not the JSON library directly.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern takes RelationConfig and extracts pattern strings internally; callers do not manipulate pattern strings — the config-to-pattern translation is at the right layer.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc documents why it intentionally does NOT call buildExploreRequest (cross-relational, not single-relation) — the asymmetry is documented rather than hidden.

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples: the local variable 'fallback' tracks whether a proximity pattern was used when the relation was not found, and the response includes a 'fallback: true/false' field — this is transparent and useful seam information.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern takes both lemma and bcqlPattern (lemma already substituted); the Javadoc explains the two-argument redundancy: lemma is needed for frequency lookup, pattern drives the search. The seam is explicit.
GrammarConfig.getRelation(String id) returns Optional<RelationConfig>, forcing callers to handle the absent case — boundary contract is honest.

Comments throughout explain WHY, not just what: GrammarConfigLoader Javadoc explains the two-tier exception contract ('Two-tier exception contract: this method intentionally wraps IOException in IllegalStateException so that startup code...'); CollocateProfileComparator explains the two-pass variance ('Second pass for variance — requires the mean — unavoidable two-pass for numerical stability').
SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc includes an ASCII-art exploration example ('Seed: house / Top adjectives: beautiful, old...') that is genuinely useful for understanding the algorithm.
HttpApiUtils class Javadoc explains why response writing and request parsing are co-located rather than split — a decision document, not a restatement.
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explains the security model (loopback-only bind) with a concrete warning for container deployment.
FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT is defined but unused. This is a mild AI hallmark: adding a DEFAULT constant for completeness without a caller that needs it.

QueryExecutor.getExecutorType() default method returns 'this.getClass().getSimpleName()' — a one-liner interface default with a single Javadoc line. This is lightweight but not clearly needed: only one implementation exists and it overrides it anyway.
No restating comments (e.g. no '// increment counter' over i++), no entry/exit debug logging on every function, no null checks on clearly non-null typed fields beyond documented seams.
No verbatim-copied boilerplate error handling: wrapWithErrorHandling is extracted and reused rather than copy-pasted.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT unused constant and QueryExecutor.getExecutorType() single-impl default are mild signs of completeness-driven additions rather than evidence-driven design.

evidence: WordSketchApiServer.java line 96: '// TODO: remove in a future version after clients have migrated to /api/semantic-field/compare.' — a live route kept for backward compatibility with no removal timeline or issue tracking reference.

The legacy alias /api/semantic-field is registered as a route alongside the canonical /api/semantic-field/compare, creating two active paths to the same handler. The Javadoc endpoint list marks it as '(legacy alias)'.

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelation(): comment says 'Canonical field name is "pattern"; cql_pattern is no longer supported' — references a removed field name. This is a historical note, not live dead code.
No @deprecated annotations on any called symbol. No DEPRECATED string markers. No 'migrate' or 'remove after' TODOs beyond the single alias.
All files are Java — no mixed JS/TS incomplete migration. No old+new API coexistence patterns (e.g. no axios+fetch, no moment+dayjs equivalents in the Java domain).
ConlluConverter was noted as recently updated (Optional→@Nullable) — no residual Optional-based methods alongside the new @Nullable pattern.
RelationType has a comment 'Relation matching strategy: surface token sequence vs. dependency annotation.' — all values are actively used; no stale members.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: The single untracked TODO for the legacy alias keeps the score below 95: it is a live endpoint deferring cleanup with no concrete exit criteria.

evidence: Top-level packages: api/, config/, exploration/, indexer/blacklab/, model/, query/, utils/, viz/ — each has a clear, single-purpose name

model/ contains pure domain DTOs (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, Edge, ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, RelationType, PosGroup, SharingCategory) with a package-info.java documenting the boundary
QueryResults.java lives in query/ but hosts result DTOs (WordSketchResult, ConcordanceResult, SnippetResult, CollocateResult) — these are the only result DTOs outside of model/
viz/ contains a single file (RadialPlot.java) which is only called from api/VisualizationHandlers — thin one-file package could be co-located in api/ without loss of clarity
utils/ has only 2 files (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator) — appropriately lean
Main.java at root level is conventional for Java entry points (high fan-out by design)
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: QueryResults.java in query/ is the sole placement outlier: other result DTOs (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult, etc.) live in model/, so result types are split across two packages. The viz/ package holds a single file only consumed by api/ — a marginal package boundary.

evidence: QueryExecutor interface: all 7 methods carry precise Javadoc (parameters, return semantics, exception contracts, and the distinction between executeCollocations vs executeSurfacePattern is explicitly documented)

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() explicitly documents its two-tier exception contract: 'this method intentionally wraps IOException in IllegalStateException so that startup code and dependency-injection containers that cannot handle checked exceptions can call it directly'
HttpApiUtils.requireParam throws IllegalArgumentException (not returns null) when param is missing; wrapWithErrorHandling maps IAE→400; this chain is documented on each method
HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams: @Nullable query → @NonNull Map return contract is annotated and documented

CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex in constructor: comment says 'Null is accepted to support test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearch. Production callers must always supply a non-null index.' — the annotation technically permits null in contexts where it will NPE

ExplorationHandlers handleSemanticFieldExplore has inline comment 'Safe: parseRelationConfig guarantees relationType is present' but this is an informal comment-guarantee rather than a type-level contract
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() is correctly annotated @Nullable and handles both cases (null when not DEP type)
contract_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: CollocateQueryHelper's @Nullable constructor parameter is the one contract whose annotation doesn't match its production semantics — it creates a documented 'null is ok for test subclasses' exception to the production rule without a type-level guardrail.

evidence: SketchHandlers.handleSketchRequest: path parsing splits on '/' and dispatches via if/else tree — each branch is distinct and the decisions (dep vs surface, single-relation vs full) are clear

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison: carries an explicit 'Handler asymmetry note' comment explaining why this handler does not call buildExploreRequest — the asymmetry is documented rather than silently introduced

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: validates 'nouns_per' before buildExploreRequest, then validates seed count after — the ordering is intentional (fail-fast on unsupported param before doing heavier validation)
GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition: single-pass bracket-depth counting to find labeled positions — the algorithm is non-trivial but documented inline
MultiSeedExplorer.identifyCommonCollocates uses `Math.min(minShared, seeds.size())` to cap threshold — clear guard against nonsensical minShared values larger than seed count
SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses a BiFunction callback to avoid duplicating the loop across handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType — good logic extraction

ConcordanceHandlers: bcqlQuery initialised to null and conditionally assigned inside an if block; a null check then activates the fallback — the null-flow is correct but the pattern introduces a mutable intermediate
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples uses a nullable mutable local (String bcqlQuery = null; ... if (rel.isPresent()) { bcqlQuery = ...; }) where a more direct Optional-map flow would be cleaner. Not blocking, but a developer tracing the null-path must verify the fallback is reachable.

evidence: JSpecify annotations (@NonNull, @Nullable) used in HttpApiUtils on all public method parameters and return types
QueryExecutor.executeCollocations declares @Nullable String lemma — matches the null-guard at the top of the implementation: 'if (lemma == null || lemma.isEmpty()) return empty'
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() declared @Nullable Path — correct, since classpath loads pass null
QueryResults.CollocateResult.collocateLemma declared @Nullable — matches the null-handling in ExploreResponseAssembler and CorpusQueryHandlers (both use null checks before using the value)
RelationConfig record uses Optional<RelationType> for relationType rather than @Nullable — a stronger type-level contract
ExploreResponseAssembler methods all annotated @NonNull on both parameters and return types
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch() declared @Nullable return — matches the null check in extractLemmaWithFallback
CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex but production code calls requireNonNull inside methods — annotation overstates what is actually safe in production
ConlluConverter.processLines returns @Nullable BufferedWriter — correctly annotated, caller handles null in finally block
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: CollocateQueryHelper's @Nullable BlackLabIndex annotation is a type-level misstatement: the annotation declares null as valid input but production paths will NPE; and WordSketchResult.pos field has no null annotation despite being set to UNKNOWN_POS sentinel in some paths.

evidence: ExplorationHandlers has 5 handler methods plus 6 private helper methods (buildExploreRequest, buildSingleSeedRequest, parseRelationConfig, parseExplorationParams, parseSeedSet, buildBaseExploreResponse) — all helpers reduce repetition without adding indirection that doesn't pay
The private records ValidatedExploreRequest and ValidatedSingleSeedRequest group validated request parameters without exposing them beyond the class — appropriate parameter grouping
ExplorationParams private record groups (top, minShared, minLogDice, nounsPerCollocate) into one object passed between helpers

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries extracts the iteration pattern into a reusable method with a BiFunction callback — avoids the copy-paste loop that previously existed between surface and dep sketches
SemanticFieldExplorer is documented as a 'coordination facade' — it owns the single-seed algorithm and delegates multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator

Mechanical signal flagged a 7-line boilerplate block shared between ExplorationHandlers.java (line 4) and CollocateProfileComparator.java (line 8) — inspection shows this is an import block, not duplicated logic; not a real design issue
ExploreResponseAssembler.roundTo2DecimalPlaces is a generic math utility residing in a response-assembly class — minor responsibility leak
design_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: ExploreResponseAssembler.roundTo2DecimalPlaces is a generic utility collocated in a response-assembly class rather than utils/. Minor but a design inconsistency in an otherwise well-factored class.

evidence: Dependency direction is well-maintained: model/ imports nothing from other domain packages; utils/ imports nothing; query/ imports only model/ and utils/; config/ imports only model/ and utils/; exploration/ imports config/, query/, and model/; api/ imports config/, exploration/, query/, and model/; Main.java imports all subsystems as the entry point
No reciprocal (bidirectional) dependencies found: model/ does not import api/, query/ does not import exploration/ or api/, config/ does not import query/ or exploration/
api/ExploreResponseAssembler was correctly moved to api/ — it imports model/ (fine) and does not leak presentation concerns back into model/
SemanticFieldExplorer imports exploration.* (its own package) plus config/, query/, model/ — no upward imports
RelationType enum lives in model/ and is referenced by both config/ (RelationConfig.relationType) and exploration/ (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) — correct direction
QueryResults lives in query/ and is imported by api/, exploration/ — both are outer layers importing from an inner layer (query/), appropriate
One note: ExplorationHandlers (in api/) imports exploration.SemanticFieldExplorer — this is expected (api orchestrates exploration); no reverse dependency

GrammarConfigSerializer lives in config/ and imports fastjson2 (a serialization library) — the config/ package now has a light dependency on fastjson2 for this serializer, though GrammarConfig and RelationConfig themselves remain free of it
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: GrammarConfigSerializer.java in config/ introduces a fastjson2 dependency into the config package. If config/ is ever used in a context that does not include the JSON library, this serializer would need to be moved. It is a minor boundary concern — the serializer is currently used only in SketchHandlers to respond to /api/relations, so it could live in api/ instead.

evidence: No module-level (class-load) side effects found — Java does not execute module-level code on import the way Python does, and no static initializer blocks (static { ... }) are present in any class
Main.java reads System.getProperty('grammar.config', ...) inside handleBlackLabServerCommand — at method invocation time, not class-load time
HttpApiUtils reads System.getProperty('cors.allow.origin', ...) inside setCorsHeader() — at call time per the Javadoc: 'reading at call time so per-test overrides take effect immediately'
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty('grammar.config', ...) at method invocation — documented as safe for DI containers

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor opens the index (I/O in constructor) — explicitly justified in Javadoc: 'opening the index is inherent to BlackLab's lifecycle model; there is no meaningful empty state for this executor'
WordSketchApiServer defers port binding to start() — documented: 'Port binding is deferred to start() so that construction and network binding are separated'
Static final constants only: FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT, QueryResults.WordSketchResult.UNKNOWN_POS — both are immutable and carry no side effects
Logger instances are all static final and use LoggerFactory.getLogger() — standard SLF4J pattern, no ordering dependencies
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: BlackLabQueryExecutor performs I/O in its constructor (opening the index) — this is documented and justified, but it means construction can throw IOException and callers cannot construct the object without a valid index. The design is intentional, but it is a constructor side-effect that cannot be avoided in tests without mocking.

evidence: 27 test files for 43 source files — strong test-to-source ratio
HandlersTest.java (440 lines) covers SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers error paths and happy paths using a MockExchange stub
WrapHandlerTest verifies IAE→400, IOException→500, RuntimeException→500 error-classification contract
WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real HTTP server on port 18080 with a stub executor — provides integration confidence for route registration and server lifecycle
MultiSeedExplorerTest and SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest cover the core exploration algorithms with stub executors
GrammarConfigLoaderTest and RelationsValidationTest cover config parsing including error cases
CollocateQueryHelperTest, BCQLParserTest, BlackLabSnippetParserTest, CqlUtilsTest cover query-layer internals
BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest is @Disabled — the real BlackLab query path is only reachable with a live index
Main.java command dispatch has no unit tests — the CLI parsing and command routing (blacklab-index, blacklab-query, server) is untested
CorpusQueryHandlers (parseBcqlRequest, body size limit, bracket complexity check) is covered indirectly via WordSketchApiServerTest but no dedicated unit test class
VisualizationHandlers is tested via RadialPlotTest (tests the underlying RadialPlot) but no handler-level unit test for the POST body parsing or size-limit enforcement path
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Main.java CLI dispatch is untested; CorpusQueryHandlers body-validation logic (size limit, bracket depth) lacks dedicated unit tests analogous to WrapHandlerTest; BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest is @Disabled meaning the real query path has no automated CI validation.

evidence: All endpoints return {"status":"ok", ...} on success and {"error":"..."} on failure — consistent top-level envelope
Error classification is uniform: IAE→400 with {"error":"Bad request: ..."}, oversized body→413, unexpected→500
/api/sketch/{lemma} and /api/sketch/{lemma}/{relation} both return {"status":"ok","lemma":...,"relations":{...}} — consistent shape for surface and dep variants
/api/semantic-field/explore uses parameter name 'seed' (singular), /api/semantic-field/explore-multi uses 'seeds' (plural) — intentional and documented in handler Javadoc

/api/semantic-field/examples uses 'adjective' and 'noun' as parameter names; /api/concordance/examples uses 'seed' and 'collocate' — these endpoints serve similar purposes (fetch corpus examples for a word pair) but have different parameter naming conventions

/api/semantic-field/examples response has 'adjective', 'noun' fields; /api/concordance/examples response has 'seed', 'collocate' fields — parallel fields with different names for the same semantic role (the queried word pair)

/api/semantic-field/compare response does not include a 'relation_type' field (cross-relational by design); /api/semantic-field/explore and /api/semantic-field/explore-multi both include 'relation_type' — documented asymmetry but a developer using both endpoints must know to check
All exploration responses include a 'parameters' sub-map echoing the request parameters — consistent pattern
QueryExecutor interface: all methods are synchronous and throw IOException — no mixed sync/async concern
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: The two 'fetch examples for a word pair' endpoints (/api/semantic-field/examples and /api/concordance/examples) use different parameter names for the same semantic roles: one uses (adjective, noun) and the other uses (seed, collocate). A developer building a client must look up each endpoint separately.

evidence: WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: 'the server binds exclusively to the loopback address (127.0.0.1) so it is never exposed on any external network interface. All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind.'
All 14 registered route handlers follow the same trust model: no authentication, no session checks, no role checks — consistent posture across all endpoints
The CORS handling is applied uniformly via registerHandler/registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler — no endpoint bypasses CORS preflight handling

The /api/bcql POST endpoint (arbitrary query execution) has consistent input validation: size limit (65536 bytes), pattern length limit (1024 chars), bracket complexity limit (20) — all enforced uniformly
No magic permission strings, role literals, or auth decorators exist anywhere in the codebase — there is nothing to be inconsistently applied
The loopback bind uses InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — not a string literal — so there is no risk of an accidental 0.0.0.0 bind from a typo

WordSketchApiServer Javadoc includes an explicit migration warning: 'Any change to the bind address (e.g., to 0.0.0.0 for container deployment) must be accompanied by an authentication or network-level access-control layer'
Input validation (HttpApiUtils.requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) is applied consistently across all request parameters in all handlers
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The security model is correct and consistently applied for a local research tool, but there is no mechanism to enforce the loopback-only constraint if the server is ever containerised or the bind address is changed by a future developer. The warning exists in Javadoc but there is no runtime assertion or test that verifies the bind address restriction.

strengths: Package layout maps precisely to domain capabilities (indexing, querying, exploration, API serving, config) — a new engineer can predict where to find a given concern.
SemanticFieldExplorer explicitly documents its coordination-facade role, making the decomposition of single-seed, multi-seed, and comparison algorithms readable at a glance.
model/package-info.java clearly articulates the two sub-categories of types and their ownership rules, making the boundary between pure DTOs and enriched model objects explicit and navigable.
Main.java follows a clean command-dispatch pattern with three focused handlers; grammar config and executor lifecycle are wired at the server startup layer only.
Public surfaces are small and consistent: handler classes are package-private, only seams (SemanticFieldExplorer, QueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader) are public.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The two issues are isolated and not systemic: a conceptual mixing of orthogonal enums in RelationType, and a single untracked legacy alias with a TODO but no timeline — neither blurs overall ownership or decomposition.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits above the 'solid but uneven' anchor (80). Package decomposition is genuinely domain-aligned, facades are explicit, and change boundaries are predictable. The RelationType enum mixing and untracked legacy alias are real but minor frictions that do not confuse ownership. A score of 88 reflects a strong architecture with a few lingering rough edges that prevent it from reaching exemplary (95+).

strengths: Consistent package-private handler classes in api/ with a uniform 'domain + Handlers' naming pattern across all five handler classes.
Consistent execute* verb family on QueryExecutor — every query method has a clear, distinct verb that matches its semantics.
All four enums use the same label/toString pattern for API-safe serialization, eliminating a common inconsistency source.
Config classes follow a uniform trio pattern: GrammarConfig (value), GrammarConfigLoader (IO), GrammarConfigSerializer (serialization) — a convention used correctly and consistently.
convention_outlier.issue_character: The one real outlier is localized: buildExploreRequest and buildSingleSeedRequest in ExplorationHandlers are near-duplicate private helpers with diverging parameter sets, which creates a subtle convention island within an otherwise uniform file.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is solidly above the 80 anchor for convention consistency — naming, organization, and enum patterns are uniform throughout. The single near-duplicate helper pair in ExplorationHandlers is a localized smell, not a systemic drift, and does not affect any other module. A score of 90 reflects a highly consistent codebase with one minor localized inconsistency.

strengths: All HTTP handlers funnel errors through a single wrapWithErrorHandling translation point — error-to-HTTP-status mapping is defined in exactly one place.
BlackLabQueryExecutor explicitly converts ErrorOpeningIndex (runtime) to IOException (checked) with a comment explaining the contract rationale.
@Nullable annotations are used consistently for nullable return values (RelationConfig.computeDeprel, QueryResults.CollocateResult.collocateLemma), giving callers clear guidance.
FetchExamplesOptions validates its constructor argument and throws IAE immediately, consistent with the rest of the codebase.
error_consistency.issue_character: The main issue is localized to GrammarConfigLoader: it reuses IOException for semantic validation errors (missing version, duplicate ID) rather than distinguishing file-IO failures from config-schema failures — a conflation that makes callers in Main.java handle both with identical catch blocks.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase is solidly above 80 on error consistency. The unified handler pipeline is a genuine strength and eliminates entire categories of inconsistency. The one real issue — IOException overloading in GrammarConfigLoader — is a known pattern in Java config loaders and does not cascade to callers in a harmful way (Main.java handles both cases identically). A score of 86 reflects a largely unified error strategy with one principled but imperfect exception-category choice.

strengths: execute*/handle*/build*/parse*/format* verb families are consistent across all production classes — no generic 'process' or 'handle' catch-all names.
Phase method names in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) read as an algorithm narrative.
Domain vocabulary is maintained end-to-end: logDice, collocate, seed, headword, lemma, deprel, bcql, cql — no leakage of technical acronyms into domain names.
Private record names (ValidatedExploreRequest, SeedCollocateData, ConcordanceExamplesRequest, ExplorationParams) are precise and reveal their scope.
naming_quality.issue_character: The two issues are isolated: one is a silent domain assumption baked into a local variable rename, and the other is a dead constant that misleads about the intended entry point.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Naming quality is well above the 80 anchor. The verb-family discipline is one of the strongest aspects of this codebase — finding a method is fast because the naming discipline predicts it. The two flagged items (ConcordanceHandlers noun/adjective assumption, unused DEFAULT constant) are minor and localized. A score of 89 reflects near-exemplary naming with two small rough edges.

strengths: QueryExecutor's single-impl status is documented honestly with an explicit note about testability over abstraction — the interface pays for itself.
GrammarConfig/Loader/Serializer three-way split cleanly distributes I/O, domain logic, and presentation concerns with no cross-contamination.
SemanticFieldExplorer's facade role is explicitly documented; delegation to MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator is purposeful and localizes algorithm ownership.
CqlUtils is a small, cohesive static utility — not a dumping ground; every method is related to CQL token manipulation.
RelationPatternBuilder extraction from RelationConfig keeps the record a pure data carrier without forcing pattern-building logic on all callers.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The issues are small and localized: FetchExamplesOptions is a marginally thin single-field wrapper, and a hardcoded inline limit in VisualizationHandlers lacks a named constant. Neither indicates systemic abstraction abuse.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The abstraction landscape is strong — interfaces are honest, facades are documented, and extractions are purposeful. The mechanical scan flagged indirection hotspots (chain depth 5 in Main.java, CollocateQueryHelper) but these are real logic chains, not gratuitous delegation. The score of 84 reflects solid, purposeful abstractions with a few minor fitness issues that prevent the 90+ range.

strengths: Exactly one library per concern: JSON (fastjson2), logging (slf4j+logback), nullability annotations (jspecify), testing (JUnit 5). No overlap.
Version properties block centralizes all version strings, preventing version drift between related artifacts (e.g. JUnit API and engine stay in sync).
Both BlackLab JARs are actively used in production code — no ghost dependencies.
jspecify is used consistently throughout the api and query layers for null-safety annotations, not just declared.
dependency_health.issue_character: The only real concern is infrastructure, not code: the BlackLab dependency is resolved from a raw GitHub URL rather than Maven Central or a hosted registry, creating a CI reliability risk.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is minimal, focused, and internally consistent — clearly above the 80 anchor. The sole material risk is the BlackLab raw-GitHub repository, which is a deployment/CI concern rather than a code design issue. The pom.xml comment acknowledges and documents the risk with mitigation options. A score of 87 reflects a lean, well-managed dependency tree with one operational risk that is documented but not yet mitigated.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer decomposes its four-phase algorithm into four focused helper methods — each reads as a single responsibility, and the main method reads as a narrative.
CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList uses a documented two-pass approach with a comment explaining why (variance requires the mean) — edge-case handling is precise, not defensive.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles bracket depth, nested brackets, and quoted strings in a clean, direct loop without over-engineering.
Named constants for limits (OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, DEFAULT_SKETCH_RESULTS, SINGLE_RELATION_RESULTS) are used consistently across query and sketch handlers.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The two issues are isolated to VisualizationHandlers: a null-reassignment pattern that deviates from the rest of the codebase, and an inline magic number where the codebase convention is named constants.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Low-level craft is strong — phase decomposition, named constants, focused helpers, and clean loops are the dominant patterns. The two issues in VisualizationHandlers are genuine deviations from the codebase's own conventions but are localized to a single file. A score of 84 reflects above-average internal craft with a small concentration of style inconsistencies in one handler class.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler centralizes all model→JSON translation for exploration endpoints, preventing scattered formatting logic across handlers.
Error propagation has a single translation point (wrapWithErrorHandling); handlers throw only domain errors (IAE, RequestEntityTooLargeException), HTTP mapping is not their concern.
Documented asymmetry in ExplorationHandlers (comparison handler does not call buildExploreRequest) makes an intentional design decision visible rather than leaving it as a surprise.
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern two-argument redundancy is documented in Javadoc with clear rationale — no hidden assumptions at the seam.
GrammarConfig.getRelation returns Optional, making the absent-relation case explicit at every call site.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are few and isolated: the main tension is that ConcordanceHandlers embeds an implicit domain assumption (noun/adjective parameter rename) at the HTTP-to-domain seam without a guard, and VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial extracts mode but never validates or documents its accepted values.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Handoff design is consistently clean across the major seams (HTTP→handler, handler→explorer, explorer→query, query→results, results→assembler→response). The two minor issues are localized to VisualizationHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers parameter handling. A score of 86 reflects handoff quality that is solid and mostly exemplary, pulled down slightly by localized seam assumptions in two handlers.

strengths: Comments are decision documents: GrammarConfigLoader's two-tier exception contract, HttpApiUtils's co-location rationale, and BlackLabQueryExecutor's two-argument redundancy explanation all add genuine insight.
No restating comments across all examined production files — every comment earns its place by explaining constraints, rationale, or non-obvious behavior.
No entry/exit logging on every function; debug logging is targeted at the algorithm phases where it reveals useful state.
Error handling is extracted into wrapWithErrorHandling rather than copy-pasted — actively anti-boilerplate.
No defensive null checks on non-nullable typed values; @NonNull annotations are used instead of defensive guards.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The minor issues are mild completeness-driven additions (an unused DEFAULT constant, a defaulted interface method overridden by the only implementation) that are characteristic of over-specification rather than meaningful debt.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: AI-generated debt indicators are largely absent from this codebase — comments explain decisions, logging is targeted, and error handling is centralized rather than copied. The two small issues (unused DEFAULT, single-impl default method) are genuine but minor. A score of 87 reflects a codebase that reads as deliberately designed, with two small indicators of completeness-driven additions.

strengths: No deprecated-but-called symbols anywhere in the production codebase.
GrammarConfigLoader explicitly documents removed field names (cql_pattern) as historical notes, not live compatibility shims.
ConlluConverter migration (Optional→@Nullable) was completed cleanly with no residual Optional patterns.
Single codebase language (Java throughout); no mixed-language migration artifacts.
Only one live legacy route exists, and it is explicitly labeled as such in both the server Javadoc and the route comment.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The only migration issue is a single legacy API route with a TODO comment but no tracking issue or removal timeline — isolated and documented, but not acted upon.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: This codebase is clean from an incomplete-migration perspective. There are no deprecated-but-called symbols, no stale migration shims that callers depend on, and no mixed-API patterns. The single legacy alias with an untracked TODO is the only open item. A score of 91 reflects a largely completed cleanup with one small, acknowledged, but untracked deferred removal.

strengths: Eight distinct packages each named for a single purpose: api (HTTP layer), config (grammar config), exploration (semantic field algorithms), query (corpus execution), model (domain DTOs), utils (stateless utilities), indexer/blacklab (index building), viz (visualization)
model/ has an explicit package-info.java describing what belongs and what does not — actionable documentation for future contributors

config/ is cleanly separated from model/: GrammarConfig and RelationConfig are pure data carriers; RelationPatternBuilder and GrammarConfigSerializer are separate pure-utility classes — no I/O mixed with data
exploration/ correctly houses three collaborating classes (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) with the public facade delegating to package-private collaborators
Main.java at the root package is the conventional Java entry point and has high fan-out to all subsystems — not a straggler
package_organization.issue_character: The only misplacements are localised: QueryResults DTOs in query/ instead of model/, and a one-file viz/ package whose sole consumer is api/; both are small and bounded.
package_organization.score_rationale: A developer navigating this codebase can predict where to find things from the directory structure alone for ~95% of cases. The api/config/exploration/query/model layering is coherent and discoverable. The package is held back from a higher score only by the QueryResults split (result DTOs scattered across two packages) and the single-file viz/ package whose placement adds navigational friction without a clear boundary benefit. Per the 90-anchor ('mostly coherent with minor placement outliers'), the codebase sits slightly below that threshold because the QueryResults outlier is a recurring discovery for anyone adding new result types.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface documents the semantic distinction between executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeBcqlQuery, and executeCqlQuery — four similar-sounding methods whose differences are precisely stated

HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling maps exceptions to HTTP status codes, and every throwing method in handler classes declares IAE for client errors — this chain is consistent and explicitly documented
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() documents its deliberate IOException→IllegalStateException wrapping with the specific audience (DI containers) that motivates it
GrammarConfig.getRelation() returns Optional<RelationConfig> rather than nullable — the Optional contract correctly signals that absent-relation is a normal case
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() is @Nullable with clear semantics: null when relation type is not DEP
contract_coherence.issue_character: The only contract gap is localised to CollocateQueryHelper's constructor, where @Nullable on a parameter that NPEs in production is a mismatch between the annotation and the actual safe-call precondition.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: A developer reading this codebase will find that the vast majority of contracts — error propagation, Optional semantics, return-type completeness, exception chaining — are precisely stated and honoured. The one recurring pattern that pulls the score below 90 is the @Nullable BlackLabIndex in CollocateQueryHelper: the annotation says null is acceptable but production code will NPE, and the only escape hatch is a constructor comment. This is a low-downstream-impact gap but it is a genuine contract mismatch. Per the 90-anchor ('minor local mismatches with low downstream impact'), the score sits just below 90.

strengths: Dispatch in SketchHandlers.handleSketchRequest is a shallow if/else tree over path segments — each decision point leads to one handler with no shared fallthrough
ExplorationHandlers documents the asymmetry of handleSemanticFieldComparison with an inline comment explaining why it deviates from the buildExploreRequest pattern — unusual control flow is annotated
Guard clauses are used consistently throughout HTTP handlers: missing params throw IAE before any business logic runs
MultiSeedExplorer.identifyCommonCollocates caps the minShared threshold at seeds.size() with a clear one-liner — edge case is handled in the obvious place
The executeRelationQueries callback approach eliminates a copy-pasted loop between surface and dependency sketch paths — single control flow implementation
logic_clarity.issue_character: The one clarity gap is a nullable mutable local in ConcordanceHandlers — an isolated pattern in a single method that a developer reading the fallback path must manually trace.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: A developer tracing logic through this codebase will find clear, documented decision branches and no duplicated logic. The handlers are well-factored: helpers like buildExploreRequest, parseExplorationParams, and executeRelationQueries reduce the cognitive load on each handler. The score is pulled below 90 by the nullable-intermediate pattern in handleConcordanceExamples, which is the one method where a developer must mentally track a mutable null variable to understand the fallback path. Per the 90-anchor ('mostly clear with isolated simplification opportunities'), the score sits just below.

strengths: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied throughout the api/ and query/ packages on public method signatures — a developer can trust annotations on API boundaries
Optional<RelationType> used in RelationConfig rather than @Nullable — stronger than annotation alone because callers are forced to handle the absent case at compile time
BlackLabSnippetParser declares @Nullable on extractLemmaFromMatch and extractLastLemma — callers use null-checks consistently
QueryExecutor.executeCollocations @Nullable String lemma matches the null-guard in the implementation without silent fallthrough
ConlluConverter.processLines @Nullable BufferedWriter return type correctly signals to the caller that no writer may exist on certain code paths
type_safety.issue_character: The type safety gaps are isolated: one constructor parameter annotation that is technically permissive beyond the safe precondition, and one record field (WordSketchResult.pos) that omits a nullability annotation.
type_safety.score_rationale: Annotations are broadly accurate and consistently applied across public API surfaces. The codebase uses Optional appropriately for Optional config fields and @Nullable for legitimately nullable returns. The score is held below 90 by the CollocateQueryHelper constructor annotation mismatch (documented elsewhere as a contract_coherence issue as well) and by WordSketchResult.pos having no nullability annotation despite the UNKNOWN_POS sentinel indicating it is never null in practice — that lack of annotation creates minor friction for callers using null-analysis tools. Per the 90-anchor, these are soft spots that 'don't cause real confusion' but are real annotation gaps.

strengths: ExplorationHandlers private records (ValidatedExploreRequest, ValidatedSingleSeedRequest, ExplorationParams) group related parameters — no multi-parameter lists that should be objects
SketchHandlers extracts the relation-query loop into executeRelationQueries with a BiFunction — eliminates a structural pattern that would otherwise be repeated across surface and dependency sketches
SemanticFieldExplorer explicitly documents its role as a coordination facade and delegates algorithms to focused classes (MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) — no god-class accumulation
ExploreResponseAssembler owns all model-to-JSON translation for exploration results — no formatting logic scattered across handler classes
MultiSeedExplorer.SeedCollocateData is a private record grouping related computed data from fetchCollocatesPerSeed — precise data grouping at the right scope
design_coherence.issue_character: The only design gap found is a single utility method (roundTo2DecimalPlaces) that belongs in utils/ but lives in a response assembler — an isolated, minor responsibility mismatch.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Functions across this codebase are focused and abstractions earn their keep. The handler decomposition pattern (handler classes → helpers → model layer) is applied consistently, parameter grouping via private records reduces long parameter lists, and algorithm responsibilities are explicitly delegated. The score is held from 90+ only by the roundTo2DecimalPlaces utility method in ExploreResponseAssembler (a generic math concern in a serialization class) and the fact that the duplication scanner flagged a 7-line window that on inspection is just a shared import pattern, not a design defect. Per the 90-anchor, this is 'mostly focused with minor multi-responsibility' — a single method is slightly out of place.

strengths: Strict unidirectional dependency flow: model ← utils ← query/config ← exploration ← api ← Main; no layer violates this order
model/ has zero outbound domain imports — it is a true dependency sink, enabling all other packages to import from it safely
RelationType enum lives in model/ rather than config/ — correctly avoiding a situation where query/ or exploration/ would need to import from config/
GrammarConfig and RelationConfig are pure data carriers with no JSON or I/O imports — serialization concern extracted to GrammarConfigSerializer
QueryExecutor is an interface in query/ — api/ and exploration/ depend on the interface, not the implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor), keeping the dependency abstract
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The only boundary concern is that GrammarConfigSerializer introduces a fastjson2 dependency into the config/ package, making config/ depend on a serialization library that its value objects (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig) do not need.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: A developer examining the dependency graph will find a clean unidirectional flow with no cycles. The layer boundaries are respected throughout, and model/ correctly serves as the dependency anchor. The score is held below 95 by GrammarConfigSerializer residing in config/ rather than api/ — this places a presentation-layer library dependency (fastjson2) in a package whose other classes are deliberately free of it. Per the 90-anchor ('mostly coherent with isolated boundary drift'), this is a single isolated drift point that does not currently cause harm but would complicate a hypothetical future use of config/ in a non-JSON context.

strengths: System.getProperty() calls are deferred to method invocation time throughout — cors.allow.origin is read per-request, grammar.config is read per-call to createDefaultEnglish() — no stale environment reads at class load
WordSketchApiServer separates construction from port binding: the constructor builds handler objects, start() binds the port — callers can configure the server between construction and start()
No static mutable singleton state: the only statics are final constants (FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT, UNKNOWN_POS) and loggers
GrammarConfigLoader is a pure static utility class: no instance state, no boot-order dependencies
CollocateQueryHelper accepts its BlackLabIndex dependency via constructor injection rather than looking it up statically
initialization_coupling.issue_character: No initialization coupling issues were found. All system property reads are deferred, there are no static mutable singletons, and construction/binding is appropriately separated. The I/O in BlackLabQueryExecutor's constructor is justified and documented.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: A developer can import and construct any class in this codebase without triggering side effects from a sibling class or requiring a specific initialization order. The one constructor that performs I/O (BlackLabQueryExecutor) does so for sound lifecycle reasons that are explicitly documented. Per the 100-anchor ('a developer can import any module without worrying about boot-order dependencies'), this codebase nearly qualifies — the only minor reservation is the constructor I/O in BlackLabQueryExecutor, which is a deliberate design choice rather than accidental coupling. The score is 94 rather than 100 because constructor I/O is still a non-zero coupling concern even when justified.

strengths: Stub-executor pattern used consistently across HandlersTest, SemanticFieldExplorerTest, MultiSeedExplorerTest, CollocateProfileComparatorTest — tests are fast and do not require a live index
WrapHandlerTest validates the entire error-classification contract (IAE→400, IOException→500, RuntimeException→500) — this critical cross-cutting behaviour has explicit test coverage
WordSketchApiServerTest starts a real HTTP server with stub executor — validates route registration, CORS headers, and server lifecycle without a real corpus
GrammarConfigLoaderTest covers both valid and invalid config cases (missing version, duplicate ids, invalid positions) — the parsing validation logic has good branch coverage
ExploreResponseAssemblerTest covers the model-to-JSON serialization path for both ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult
test_strategy.issue_character: Gaps are concentrated at two points: the Main.java CLI layer (zero unit tests) and CorpusQueryHandlers body-validation logic (no dedicated handler-level tests beyond the server integration test).
test_strategy.score_rationale: The codebase has strong coverage for the exploration and query algorithm paths, the error handling contract, and grammar config parsing. The test portfolio uses appropriate techniques (stub executors, real server with stub, dedicated contract tests). The score is pulled below 85 by three gaps: Main.java CLI dispatch is completely untested (if a developer breaks the argument parsing or adds a new command, there is no safety net), CorpusQueryHandlers validation paths are only incidentally covered, and BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest being @Disabled means the full real-index path is never automatically validated. Per the 80-anchor, a developer would 'worry about making changes in certain areas' — specifically the CLI layer and the BCQL body-parsing handler.

strengths: Consistent {"status":"ok"} success envelope and {"error":"..."} error envelope across all endpoints
All exploration-family endpoints (/explore, /explore-multi, /compare) include a 'parameters' sub-map echoing the resolved request parameters — clients can inspect what defaults were applied
The explore vs explore-multi parameter naming (seed vs seeds) is intentional and documented in handler Javadoc — the distinction is a feature, not drift
All endpoints are synchronous GET or POST (no mixed async); error classification is uniform across all handlers
QueryExecutor interface has consistent method signatures: all take (lemma, ..., minLogDice, maxResults) in that order for collocate-retrieval methods
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The main inconsistency is localised to two sibling 'fetch examples' endpoints that use different parameter names for the same semantic roles — an API-client developer must read both endpoint docs to discover the naming difference.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API surface is mostly coherent: a developer who has seen one endpoint can predict the envelope shape, error format, and parameter pattern for most others. The primary friction point is the parameter naming split between /api/semantic-field/examples (adjective, noun) and /api/concordance/examples (seed, collocate) — two endpoints that serve the same user story (show corpus examples for a word pair) but expose different parameter vocabularies. This creates a 'why is this different?' moment that pushes the score below 85. The absence of relation_type in /api/semantic-field/compare is a documented asymmetry with a sound reason, which is less penalising. Per the 80-anchor, the developer would 'mostly be well-served but would hit recurring friction — moments of why is this different?'

strengths: Security model is explicitly documented in WordSketchApiServer class Javadoc — a developer reading the code understands the auth posture before writing any handler
All endpoints share exactly the same trust model (unauthenticated, loopback-only) — there are no sibling routes with inconsistent auth patterns
CORS handling is applied uniformly via the registerHandler infrastructure — no endpoint can bypass it
InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() is used programmatically rather than as a string literal — reduces the risk of an accidental bind-address change
The /api/bcql endpoint's input validation (size limit, complexity limit) is consistent with how other POST handlers validate bodies
authorization_consistency.issue_character: No authorization inconsistencies exist — the security model is uniformly unauthenticated with documented rationale. The only concern is the lack of a runtime or test-level enforcement of the loopback-only bind.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: A developer auditing this codebase for auth consistency will find a uniform, explicitly documented posture: all endpoints are unauthenticated, loopback-only, with consistent CORS and input-validation patterns. There are no partially-auth'd siblings, no magic permission strings, and no trust boundary violations. The score is held below 95 because the loopback-only bind guarantee exists only in Javadoc and runtime code — a future developer who changes the bind address (for containerisation) would need to read the Javadoc warning to know they must add auth; there is no test or assertion that would fail to alert them. Per the 90-anchor, this is 'mostly consistent with limited, documented exceptions' — actually even stronger than that, as there are no exceptions at all, just a future-proofing gap.

issues.summary: RelationType enum conflates matching-strategy variants (SURFACE, DEP) with semantic-role labels (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF)
issues.suggestion: Split into two enums: MatchingStrategy { SURFACE, DEP } for the query-execution dimension, and SemanticRelationType { ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF } for the domain-semantic dimension. Update RelationConfig fields and callers accordingly.
evidence: RelationType.java declares: SURFACE, DEP, ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF in a single flat enum.
SketchHandlers uses RelationType.SURFACE and RelationType.DEP as dispatch keys for relation type filtering.
RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern takes RelationType... primaryTypes and matches against ADJ_MODIFIER, ADJ_PREDICATE etc.
The two uses (matching strategy vs semantic role) are independent concerns that happen to share the same enum namespace.

issues.summary: Legacy /api/semantic-field alias has an untracked TODO with no issue reference or removal timeline
issues.suggestion: Add a deprecation marker in the Javadoc comment and link to a tracking issue. If no external consumers are known, set a removal milestone or add a deprecation HTTP header (e.g., Deprecation: true) to signal the alias status to callers.
evidence: Line 96: '// TODO: remove in a future version after clients have migrated to /api/semantic-field/compare.' with no issue number.
The legacy path is registered as a live route, creating an undocumented alias that diverges from the stated public surface in the server Javadoc.

issues.summary: buildExploreRequest and buildSingleSeedRequest in ExplorationHandlers are near-duplicate helpers that differ by only one field
issues.suggestion: Merge the two private records into a single ValidatedExploreRequest that carries an optional single seed vs a seeds string (or a List<String> seeds), and unify the two builder methods into one that accepts a boolean isSingleSeed or a seedParamName String. Alternatively, inline the common steps into a shared helper method extracting just RelationConfig + ExplorationParams.
evidence: buildSingleSeedRequest reads seed, calls parseRelationConfig(params) and parseExplorationParams(params), returns ValidatedSingleSeedRequest(seed, config, params).
buildExploreRequest reads seedsRaw, calls parseRelationConfig(params) and parseExplorationParams(params), returns ValidatedExploreRequest(params, seedsRaw, config, exploreParams).
Both private records have the same RelationConfig and ExplorationParams fields; only the seed field differs (single String vs multi-seed String).
The two builders share identical body structure except for the seed-extraction call (requireParam 'seed' vs requireParam 'seeds').

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException for semantic validation failures (duplicate ID, missing version), conflating schema errors with I/O failures
issues.suggestion: Replace semantic validation throws inside parse() with IllegalArgumentException (or a dedicated ConfigParseException extends IllegalArgumentException). Keep IOException only for file-reading failures. In Main.java and GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish(), catch IAE/ConfigParseException separately to give users a clearer 'invalid config' vs 'can't read file' message.
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.parseAndValidateVersion(): 'throw new IOException("Config error: Missing version field...")' — not an I/O failure.
GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelations(): 'throw new IOException("Config error: Duplicate relation id: " + config.id())' — not an I/O failure.
GrammarConfigLoader.validatePositions(): 'throw new IOException("Config error: Relation positions...")' — not an I/O failure.
Main.java catch block: 'catch (IOException e)' handles both file-not-found and schema-validation errors identically, giving no differentiation.

issues.summary: ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest silently renames 'seed' param to 'noun' and 'collocate' to 'adjective', baking in a domain assumption
issues.suggestion: Rename the local variables and record fields to match the HTTP parameter names: 'headword' (from 'seed') and 'collocate' (keep). If the endpoint is adjective-specific by design, document that constraint in the Javadoc and reject non-adjective relation types explicitly.
evidence: parseConcordanceExamplesRequest: 'String noun = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(params, "seed");' and 'String adjective = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(params, "collocate");'
The ConcordanceExamplesRequest record uses 'String noun' and 'String adjective' fields even though the HTTP contract uses 'seed' and 'collocate' as the generic terms.
The endpoint could be used for non-adjective collocates (e.g. verb collocates), but the local name 'adjective' implies a type constraint that is not enforced.

issues.summary: FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT constant is defined but never used in production code
issues.suggestion: Either remove DEFAULT if it serves no purpose, or use it in callers where 10 is the intended default (e.g. replace 'new FetchExamplesOptions(HttpApiUtils.parseIntParam(params, "top", 10))' with conditional use of DEFAULT when the param is absent).
evidence: FetchExamplesOptions.java: 'public static final FetchExamplesOptions DEFAULT = new FetchExamplesOptions(10);'
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples: 'new FetchExamplesOptions(maxExamples)' is called with a parsed param, not DEFAULT.
No other production call site references FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT.

issues.summary: FetchExamplesOptions is a single-field wrapper record with an unused DEFAULT constant that adds indirection without leverage
issues.suggestion: Inline int maxExamples directly into fetchExamples() signature. If forward-compatibility is needed in future, introduce the options record then. Remove the unused DEFAULT constant in either case.
evidence: FetchExamplesOptions contains exactly one field: int maxExamples. Its Javadoc cites forward-compatibility as the rationale.
SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples takes @NonNull FetchExamplesOptions opts, then calls opts.maxExamples() once.
FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT is defined but no caller uses it.
Contrast with SingleSeedExplorationOptions which composes ExplorationOptions + nounsPerCollocate — a genuine two-concern combination that justifies a wrapper record.

issues.summary: BlackLab 4.x is resolved from a raw GitHub URL with no availability SLA, creating CI reliability risk
issues.suggestion: Install the BlackLab JARs into a project-local Maven repository (committed to source control or a CI artifact store) as a one-time setup step, and remove the raw-GitHub repository entry from pom.xml. Alternatively, mirror to a private registry and update the repository URL. Either eliminates the external URL dependency from the build.
evidence: pom.xml: '<url>https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/</url>' for the blacklab repository.
pom.xml comment: 'NOTE: BlackLab 4.x is not published to Maven Central (latest on Central is 3.0.1). This URL has no SLA and can become unavailable (rate-limited, branch deleted, or repo renamed).'
The comment lists install-blacklab.sh and private registry mitigations but neither is implemented in the current CI/build workflow.

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial uses a null-check-then-reassign pattern instead of the null-coalescing idiom used elsewhere
issues.suggestion: Replace with 'String center = Objects.requireNonNullElse(obj.getString("center"), "");' or 'String center = Optional.ofNullable(obj.getString("center")).orElse("");' for consistency with the codebase pattern.
evidence: 'String center = obj.getString("center"); if (center == null) center = "";' — two statements that could be one.
The rest of the codebase uses Objects.requireNonNull, Optional, or @Nullable + guard clause patterns consistently.
Contrast: HttpApiUtils uses '@NonNull' return type and throws on missing param; ExplorationHandlers uses Objects.requireNonNull in constructor.

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial hardcodes item limit 40 inline without a named constant
issues.suggestion: Extract 'private static final int MAX_RADIAL_ITEMS = 40;' and replace the inline literal.
evidence: 'int limit = Math.min(40, itemsArr.size());' — 40 is the maximum radial plot items, inlined with no named constant.
SketchHandlers uses 'private static final int DEFAULT_SKETCH_RESULTS = 20;' and 'private static final int SINGLE_RELATION_RESULTS = 50;' for equivalent limits.
CollocateQueryHelper uses 'private static final int OVER_FETCH_FACTOR = 3;'.

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers extracts a 'mode' parameter from the POST body but never validates or documents accepted values
issues.suggestion: Add accepted-values documentation to the endpoint Javadoc (e.g. mode: 'spiral' | 'radial' | null-for-default). Add a validation guard at the handler boundary that throws IAE for unrecognized mode values, consistent with how other optional enum-style parameters are handled in the codebase.
evidence: VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial: 'String mode = obj.getString("mode");' — extracted but not validated; null is passed to RadialPlot.renderFromItems.
RadialPlot.renderFromItems accepts a String mode parameter; the accepted values and null behavior are not documented at the handler seam.
A caller sending an unsupported mode value would receive no error; the output may be silently wrong.

issues.summary: FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT is a completeness-driven constant that no production caller uses
issues.suggestion: Remove DEFAULT unless a caller genuinely uses it. If the intent is to signal the default value, document it in the Javadoc of the record and use it in ExplorationHandlers when the 'top' param is absent.
evidence: FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT = new FetchExamplesOptions(10) declared public static final.
ExplorationHandlers creates 'new FetchExamplesOptions(maxExamples)' instead of DEFAULT even when maxExamples defaults to 10.
No test or production code references FetchExamplesOptions.DEFAULT.

issues.summary: QueryExecutor.getExecutorType() is a default interface method that the sole implementation overrides, making the default unreachable
issues.suggestion: Remove the default implementation and make getExecutorType() an abstract method, or remove the method entirely if it is only used for logging (where the class name from logger is already available).
evidence: QueryExecutor.getExecutorType() default: 'return this.getClass().getSimpleName();'
BlackLabQueryExecutor overrides it: 'return "blacklab";'
The default implementation is never reached because the only production class overrides it. The default adds interface surface without leverage.

issues.summary: Legacy /api/semantic-field alias has a migration TODO with no tracking issue, timeline, or deprecation signal to clients
issues.suggestion: Either (a) set a concrete removal version in the comment and add a 'Deprecation: true' HTTP response header to the legacy route so clients can detect the deprecation, or (b) if no external callers are known, remove the route now. Link to a tracking issue so the removal does not drift further.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer.java line 95-98: 'Legacy alias for /api/semantic-field/compare — kept for backward compatibility. TODO: remove in a future version after clients have migrated to /api/semantic-field/compare.'
The route is live and functional — no Deprecation header or warning response field to signal callers.
No GitHub issue number or version milestone referenced in the comment.

issues.summary: QueryResults DTO classes live in query/ while all other result DTOs live in model/
issues.suggestion: Move QueryResults.java to model/ and update the ~8 import sites. Staged plan: (1) move the file, (2) run `mvn compile` to identify all broken imports, (3) fix imports — primarily in query/, api/, and exploration/ packages. Validate with `mvn test`.
evidence: QueryResults.java in query/ defines WordSketchResult, ConcordanceResult, SnippetResult, CollocateResult — all pure data records
AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult all live in model/ with an explicit package-info.java
api/ExploreResponseAssembler imports both query.QueryResults and model.* — a developer adding result types must check both packages

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex but production callers must not pass null
issues.suggestion: Remove @Nullable from the constructor parameter and add a Objects.requireNonNull guard in the constructor body (or keep the existing requireNonNull calls in the methods). For test subclasses that need to pass null, use a package-private factory method or a dedicated test subclass that overrides the constructor, avoiding the annotation mismatch entirely.
evidence: Constructor: `CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index)` — @Nullable annotation declares null as a valid argument
getTotalFrequency calls `Objects.requireNonNull(index, "BlackLabIndex must not be null for getTotalFrequency")` — NPE on null in production

Comment in constructor: 'Null is accepted to support test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearch. Production callers must always supply a non-null index.' — the @Nullable annotation is used as a test-seam mechanism rather than a true nullability contract

issues.summary: handleConcordanceExamples uses a null-initialized mutable local to decide fallback, obscuring the decision path
issues.suggestion: Refactor to use Optional.map or a ternary: `String bcqlQuery = grammarConfig.getRelation(req.relation()).map(rel -> RelationPatternBuilder.buildFullPattern(rel, req.noun())).map(p -> CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition(p, req.adjective(), grammarConfig.getRelation(req.relation()).get().collocatePosition())).orElse(null);` — or extract to a private method `resolveOrNullBcqlQuery(req)` that returns Optional<String>, then the fallback branch is a clear orElseGet.
evidence: `String bcqlQuery = null;` followed by `if (rel.isPresent()) { ... bcqlQuery = ...; }` followed by `if (bcqlQuery == null || bcqlQuery.isEmpty()) { ... fallback = true; }` — the logic depends on a null sentinel to detect the 'relation not resolved' case
A developer reading the method must track two variables (bcqlQuery and fallback) to understand when the fallback activates

issues.summary: WordSketchResult.pos field carries no @NonNull or @Nullable annotation despite having a UNKNOWN_POS sentinel constant
issues.suggestion: Add @NonNull to the pos field: `public record WordSketchResult(String lemma, @NonNull String pos, ...)`. This documents the invariant (callers use UNKNOWN_POS sentinel, not null) and lets null-analysis tools enforce it at construction sites.
evidence: `public record WordSketchResult(String lemma, String pos, ...)` — pos has no nullability annotation
`public static final String UNKNOWN_POS = "unknown";` sentinel exists on the record, implying pos is always non-null (never null, just potentially 'unknown')
ExploreResponseAssembler and SketchHandlers use pos without null checks, consistent with the non-null assumption

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.roundTo2DecimalPlaces is a generic math utility misplaced in a serialization class
issues.suggestion: Move roundTo2DecimalPlaces to utils/MathUtils.java (new file) or add it to LogDiceCalculator.java if the concern fits. Update the 6 call sites inside ExploreResponseAssembler to use the new location. Run `mvn test` to validate.
evidence: `public static double roundTo2DecimalPlaces(double value)` in ExploreResponseAssembler — a pure math function with no dependency on exploration results or API concerns
LogDiceCalculator already lives in utils/ and is the established home for math utilities in this codebase
